key_departures.impact: 10-year franchise LT requested and received his release after contract renegotiation talks broke down. His release creates a wide-open starting hole at left tackle opposite Penei Sewell. Detroit may shift Sewell from RT to LT, but a new OT starter is still needed. Saves $11.6M in cap space with $9.4M in dead money.

Was Detroit's second-leading pass rusher with 11 sacks and a career-high 53 pressures in 2025. His departure leaves Aidan Hutchinson with no established complementary rusher. EDGE is now arguably the most critical need on the roster.

5-year Lion signed a 2yr/$17M deal in Tampa. Was the heartbeat of the defense and the primary WILL/signal-caller alongside Jack Campbell. His departure creates a depth/quality hole at LB, though Detroit retains Campbell, Derrick Barnes, and now Rodriguez.

Signed 2yr/$16M with Washington. Was one of the most-used CBs in Detroit's injury-riddled secondary in 2025, effectively starting for much of the season. His departure thins a CB group that is still recovering from multiple injuries.

Signed 2-year deal to return to Arizona. Had 2 sacks in his lone season in Detroit and was considered the best interior run-defender in the rotation. His departure thins an IDL group already under pressure with DJ Reader also departing.

The veteran nose tackle appears unlikely to return to Detroit per multiple reports. Was a starter-caliber run-stopper. His potential departure alongside Lopez's exit significantly depletes the IDL room. Detroit now relies heavily on Alim McNeill, Tyleik Williams, and Levi Onwuzurike.

Traded to Houston for OL Juice Scruggs, a 2026 4th-round pick, and a 2027 7th-round pick. Was Jahmyr Gibbs's backfield partner and the team's power back. His departure creates a need for a complementary RB2 behind Gibbs.
Signed a 2yr deal with Buffalo. Was Jared Goff's backup. Departure creates a minor need at QB2 that was quickly addressed with Bridgewater's re-signing.
Signed 1yr/$5.1M with Chicago. Five-year Lion was a reliable WR4/special teams ace. His departure is a depth loss only; the Lions retain Amon-Ra St. Brown, Jameson Williams, and Isaac TeSlaa.

Cut to save $5.6M in cap space. Was pressed into starting center duty after Frank Ragnow's retirement and struggled. His release was necessary and the IOL center vacancy was the primary driver behind signing Cade Mays.
Signed 2yr/$5.1M with the Rams. Was a core special-teamer and depth LB. Minor loss, doesn't change the need picture significantly.

signings.analysis: 3yr/$25M with $14M guaranteed directly fills the starting center void left by Graham Glasgow/Frank Ragnow retirement. Mays allowed zero sacks over 726 snaps with Carolina in 2025 and has a 94.3% pass block win rate. At $8.3M AAV, this is a bargain-tier deal for a projected starter at a position that was tier-1 critical. The three-year commitment signals Detroit's intention to make him the long-term answer. IOL drops from critical to a depth/developmental need for guard depth only.

Acquired via the David Montgomery trade (with 2026 4th-round pick and 2027 7th-round pick). Former 2nd-round pick has one year remaining on his rookie deal. Primarily played LG in Houston with some center reps in 2024. Graded poorly in 2025 (45.0 PFF overall, 78th of 81 qualifying guards). Depth piece and camp competition, not a starter. Does not move the IOL needle beyond what Mays already addressed.

1yr/$5M deal. Has 38 career starts, including 11 at RT for Miami in 2025. Can play either tackle spot. Has starting experience and functional value, but a 1-year deal at $5M is below starter money for OT. He is the Lions' bridge/insurance option while they figure out the Penei Sewell LT/RT alignment and potentially draft a tackle. Does not fully solve the starting OT need created by Decker's release, especially at LT.

1yr/$1.81M prove-it deal. A Super Bowl winner with the Chiefs who is a physical north-south runner in the Montgomery mold — ideal stylistic replacement behind Jahmyr Gibbs. However, the 1-year minimum-adjacent deal reflects injury concerns and a down stretch of play. His ability to stay healthy and recapture earlier form is legitimately in doubt. The deal is essentially cost-free but also commitment-free. RB goes from a tier-1 need (post-trade) to a partially-addressed tier-2 need with ongoing risk.

1-year deal to return as Goff's backup. Previously served as Goff's backup in 2023 and returned for the late-season playoff stretch in 2024-25. Trusted by Dan Campbell and understands the system deeply. The QB2 need created by Kyle Allen's departure is considered resolved — this is a clear backup role with no starter implications.

1yr/$4M re-sign. Was one of Detroit's more effective secondary signings last year, providing versatility and depth amid a wave of injuries. His return is a continuity depth move — he knows the system and provides insurance. However, the 1-year, sub-starter money deal signals this is a CB3/depth role, not the solution to the Robertson departure. CB remains a genuine need.

1-year deal, terms undisclosed. A versatile DB who has played nickel CB and safety for Tampa, recording 165 tackles over three seasons. His 2025 season saw limited action (25 tackles in 14 games). With Brian Branch (Achilles) and Kerby Joseph (knee) both recovering from season-ending injuries, Izien provides emergency insurance and ST value but is not a legitimate starter. Safety remains a significant concern entering the draft.

1-year deal, terms undisclosed. Former Titans 2nd-round pick (No. 35 overall) with 38 career starts. Split 2025 between Tennessee and the Rams, posting a 69.2 PFF overall grade. Projects as the primary nickel/slot corner and will likely compete for a starting role. At just 26 with significant starting experience, this is the most upside-driven depth signing Detroit has made in the secondary. Still a 1-year deal with no commitment, so CB is partially but not fully addressed — Detroit should still target a CB in the draft.

1-year deal, terms undisclosed. Returning from a torn ACL that limited him to 7 games in 2025. Re-signing provides depth and a familiar face in the system, but he is not Alex Anzalone's replacement as a starter/signal-caller. Detroit still lacks an established WILL linebacker capable of replacing Anzalone's role. LB remains a meaningful draft and/or free agency need.

1-year deal, terms undisclosed. Veteran of 8 NFL seasons with 127 career appearances and connection to new OC Drew Petzing from their time together in Minnesota. Sam LaPorta is recovering from a back injury, and Brock Wright (neck) is also working back. Conklin provides experienced TE2/TE3 insurance in a new 13-personnel heavy scheme. Given all three of Detroit's tight ends are in the final year of their contracts, TE is still a position that needs a long-term answer — likely in the draft.

Re-signed as an RFA. The longest-tenured Lion enters his 8th season with the team as a WR4/ST contributor. Originally signed as a UDFA in 2019, provides zero impact on the WR need picture. With St. Brown, Jameson Williams, and Isaac TeSlaa ahead of him, Kennedy is camp depth / ST value only.
Returned after Detroit declined to tender him as an RFA. Depth LB with special teams value. Does not meaningfully address the LB need created by Anzalone's departure.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Cade Mays signing (3yr/$25M, $14M guaranteed) directly fills the center void. He is a projected Day 1 starter with a 94.3% pass block win rate and zero sacks allowed in 2025. The three-year deal signals commitment. Juice Scruggs adds quality depth. IOL drops from critical to a developmental/guard-depth need only.

Al-Quadin Muhammad (11 sacks, 53 pressures) departed for Tampa Bay and was NOT replaced. Detroit currently has only Aidan Hutchinson and undeveloped rookie Ahmad Hassanein at the position. This is the single biggest unaddressed need on the roster and remains the highest draft/free agency priority. EDGE stays tier 1 critical.

Taylor Decker's surprise release created a new starting hole at LT. Larry Borom (1yr/$5M) is a functional depth piece but not a starter-tier solution. The Lions may shift Penei Sewell to LT, but even then a quality RT is needed. OT escalates from tier 2 to tier 1 — this is now a premium draft need, likely round 1 or 2.

Alex Anzalone's departure to Tampa escalated this need, but the Malcolm Rodriguez re-sign (depth only, recovering from torn ACL) does not offset it meaningfully. Detroit retains Jack Campbell and Derrick Barnes as starters, but lacks a quality third LB and a system-savvy WILL body. LB stays tier 2 — best addressed in rounds 3-5 of the draft or mid-tier free agency.

Amik Robertson's departure (2yr/$16M to Washington) was a meaningful loss. Rock Ya-Sin (1yr/$4M re-sign) and Roger McCreary (1yr, terms undisclosed) partially offset this with depth/rotational players and one credible nickel option in McCreary. However, both are 1-year deals with no long-term commitment. Detroit still needs a CB with multi-year starter potential. CB stays tier 2, particularly for a corner opposite DJ Reed.

Brian Branch (Achilles) and Kerby Joseph (knee) are both returning from season-ending injuries with unclear timelines. Christian Izien (1yr, depth) provides minimal insurance. DJ Reed is returning. This secondary remains fragile from an injury-risk standpoint. Safety stays tier 2 — the Lions should target a developmental S in the draft.

Roy Lopez left for Arizona and DJ Reader is likely not returning, leaving Alim McNeill, Tyleik Williams (developing 2nd-year player), and Levi Onwuzurike (returning from IR). The IDL group is thinner than last year. Williams' development is a reason for optimism, but depth is a real concern. IDL stays tier 2 — a rotational/depth DT in the draft (rounds 3-5) is warranted.

David Montgomery's trade created a RB2 need. Isiah Pacheco (1yr/$1.81M) partially addresses this with a physical complementary back in the Montgomery mold, but the 1-year deal at near-minimum salary reflects real injury risk and uncertainty. Gibbs will shoulder more load. Detroit should still target a RB in mid rounds of the draft as insurance and developmental depth.

Sam LaPorta (back) and Brock Wright (neck) are both recovering from injuries. Tyler Conklin (1yr) adds experienced depth but is a short-term solution. All three of Detroit's TEs are in the final year of their contracts. A developmental TE in the mid-to-late rounds of the draft would be prudent for long-term planning, but TE is not a pressing need for 2026 given LaPorta's upside.
Teddy Bridgewater re-signed on a 1-year deal as the established backup to Jared Goff. He knows the system and is trusted by the coaching staff. QB2 need resolved. No change needed.

key_additions.headline: 26-year-old center fills critical void left by Ragnow retirement/Glasgow struggles; allowed zero sacks in 726 snaps in 2025
Former 2nd-round pick adds interior OL depth and camp competition at guard/center
Versatile tackle veteran (38 career starts) provides swing tackle insurance after Taylor Decker release
Physical KC Super Bowl winner steps into David Montgomery's RB2 role on a prove-it deal
Former top-35 pick (26) with 38 career starts projects as starting nickelback and best secondary addition
Versatile veteran re-signs to provide secondary depth amid ongoing injury concerns at S and CB
Versatile DB/nickel hybrid adds secondary depth while Branch and Joseph recover from season-ending injuries
Fan favorite 'Rodrigo' re-signs; returning from torn ACL, gives Lions depth behind Campbell and Barnes
Michigan native and OC Drew Petzing's former Viking reunion adds veteran TE depth behind injured LaPorta
Familiar face returns as Goff's backup after Kyle Allen signed with Buffalo; mentor role in locker room

key_losses.headline: Surprise release of 10-year franchise LT after contract talks broke down; creates critical OT need and $9.4M dead cap
Second-leading pass rusher (11 sacks, 53 pressures) departs, leaving Hutchinson without an established complement
5-year Lions leader and defensive signal-caller joins division rival Tampa; major character and production loss
Versatile CB who effectively started in 2025 amid injury chaos departs, deepening secondary concerns
'Knuckles' trades out after 2,506 career rushing yards in Detroit; Lions recoup OL depth and draft capital
Best interior run-defender returns to Arizona, thinning a DT room already losing DJ Reader
5-year Lion and special teams ace reunites with Ben Johnson in Chicago; depth loss only
